summary: Well-scoped, surgically targeted plan that replaces only the weakest part of the existing shader pipeline (sine-hash layeredNoise) with coherent 3D simplex noise while preserving all other displacement layers. No new VisualParams, no new dependencies, no architectural changes. The blast radius is confined to 4 existing files plus 2 new files.
strengths: Correctly identifies layeredNoise() (lines 31-40 in both shaders) as the sole replacement target — all other displacement layers (radial expansion, twist, drift, jitter, pointer repulsion, breathing) are preserved verbatim
Derives uDisplacementScale from existing motionAmplitude * structureComplexity — no VisualParams expansion needed, and prefers-reduced-motion + device capability scaling come for free
Reuses existing uCadence uniform for noise evolution speed — artistically coherent (nighttime = slower drift) with zero new plumbing
String concatenation via Vite ?raw import is the correct pattern — already used by both pointCloud.ts (line 6) and ribbonField.ts (line 6) for their vertex shaders
Pure GLSL simplex noise (no texture lookups) maintains WebGL 1 compatibility and avoids 3D texture setup complexity
Quality tier integration is automatic — existing uNoiseOctaves (1/2/3 from quality.ts) directly controls fbm3 octave count

risks.risk: Simplex noise GLSL chunk (~120-150 lines) prepended to each shader increases compilation time on low-end GPUs
risks.mitigation: Only compiled once at init, not per-frame. Low-tier devices use 1 octave which exercises minimal code paths. WebGL shader compilation is cached by driver.

risks.risk: Bass macro noise layer (step 2) adds a second radial displacement pass on top of existing radial expansion — cumulative displacement could push points outside camera frustum
risks.mitigation: Plan scales the new layer by 0.3 factor and caps via uDisplacementScale (motionAmplitude * structureComplexity ≤ 1.0). Visual tuning in step 5/7 can further attenuate. Recommend clamping total radial displacement in the shader as a safety net.

risks.risk: fbm3() with dynamic octave loop (int uniform) may cause branch divergence warnings on some mobile GPUs
risks.mitigation: Existing layeredNoise already uses identical branching pattern (if uNoiseOctaves >= 2/3). No regression from current behavior.

risks.risk: No #version directive collision — current shaders omit #version, so prepending simplex3d.glsl is safe. If a future story adds #version 300 es, the concatenation order would break.
risks.mitigation: Document in the GLSL chunk that it must appear before any #version directive, or switch to Three.js onBeforeCompile injection if ES3 is needed later.

suggestions.description: Add a max displacement clamp in the vertex shader after all displacement layers: `pos = mix(position * uRadialScale, pos, clamp(length(pos - position * uRadialScale) / maxDisplacement, 0.0, 1.0))` to prevent points from escaping the frustum under extreme bass + high motionAmplitude.

Step 2 mentions 'uNoiseFrequency' and 'uNoiseOctaves' in the fbm3 call but these are existing uniforms with established semantics (noiseFrequency controls the layeredNoise spatial scale). Ensure fbm3 uses these identically — the plan's description is consistent but verify the GLSL implementation matches.

Geometry deformation occurs in a ShaderM.notes: Already satisfied by US-032/US-034. Steps 2-3 enhance, step 6 test #1 verifies.

Bass energy affects large-scale displace.notes: New bass-driven simplex FBM layer displaced along radial direction. Existing radial expansion preserved as additive. Step 6 test #2 verifies.

Treble energy affects fine-grain displac.notes: Existing treble jitter preserved. Slow-modulation section upgraded to simplex adds coherent fine-grain variation. Step 6 test #3 verifies.

Deformation evolves over time (not stati.notes: Noise coordinates drift via uTime * 0.00005 * uCadence. Step 6 test #4 verifies different uTime values produce different noise fields.
